title: Craft.js Architecture: State, Events, Real-Time Patterns
topic: Craft.js open-source page builder — state management, event handling, and subscription patterns applicable to Vector Git integration
summary: Craft.js uses Immer-based immutable state, declarative action/query separation, and subscription-based pub/sub for real-time UI updates. Patterns applicable to Git graph visualization and multi-repo aggregation.
content:
Contents
  1. Overview
  2. State Management (Immer + Immutability)
  3. Action/Query Separation
  4. Subscription Model (Pub/Sub)
  5. Event Handling & Dispatch
  6. Application to Vector Git Integration
  7. Sources

Overview

Craft.js is an open-source page builder framework for React. Its architecture offers clean patterns for state management, event dispatch, and real-time UI updates that align with Vector's needs for Git graph visualization and multi-repo aggregation.

Key insight: Craft separates state mutations (actions) from state queries (read-only views), uses Immer for immutability without cloning overhead, and implements a pub/sub subscription model for efficient incremental updates. All three patterns are directly applicable to Vector's Git integration.

State Management (Immer + Immutability)

Craft's Approach

Craft uses Immer.js under the hood, combined with a custom useMethods hook. The state is immutable at the React level, but mutations are written as if mutating a draft object.

// From store.tsx
export const editorInitialState: EditorState = {
  nodes: {},
  events: {
    dragged: new Set<NodeId>(),
    selected: new Set<NodeId>(),
    hovered: new Set<NodeId>(),
  },
  indicator: null,
  options: { ... },
};

When an action mutates state, Immer tracks the changes and produces a minimal patch. This enables:

  • Efficient subscribers: Only components subscribed to changed parts re-render (not the whole tree).
  • History/undo support: Patches are invertible, so undo/redo becomes trivial.
  • Declarative mutations: Developers write imperative mutations (mutate the draft), but the runtime enforces immutability guarantees.

Why This Matters for Vector

Git graph visualization requires real-time updates when commits arrive. If the entire graph re-renders on each push event, performance degrades with large repos (1000+ commits). Immer's patch-based approach means:

  • New commit arrives → Immer produces a minimal patch (e.g., "add node 5f3a to graph, link it to parent abc123").
  • Only the affected visual nodes re-render (the new commit + its parent branch line).
  • The rest of the graph stays stable, no flickering.

Action/Query Separation

Craft's Approach

Craft separates mutable state actions from read-only query methods.

// actions.ts: Methods that mutate state
const Methods = (state: EditorState, query: QueryMethods) => ({
  addNodeTreeToParent: (tree, parentId, addNodeType) => { ... },
  deleteNode: (nodeId) => { ... },
  selectNode: (nodeId) => { ... },
  // ... mutations write directly to the draft state
});

// query.tsx: Read-only methods
export const QueryMethods = {
  getNodes: () => state.nodes,
  getSelectedNodeIds: () => [...state.events.selected],
  getNode: (id) => state.nodes[id],
  // ... never mutate state
};

Store exposes both:

export type EditorStore = SubscriberAndCallbacksFor<
  typeof ActionMethodsWithConfig,
  typeof QueryMethods
>;

Why This Matters for Vector

Git integration has two distinct roles:

  • Webhook listeners (mutate state): Receive commit events from GitHub, add/update nodes in the graph.
  • UI renderers (query state): Read the graph and display it, filter by branch, search by author.
Separating actions and queries ensures:
  • Type safety: UI code cannot accidentally mutate the graph.
  • Testability: Query methods are pure (no side effects), easy to unit test.
  • Performance: Queries can be memoized without worrying about mutations.

    • Subscription Model (Pub/Sub)

    Craft's Approach

    From useMethods.ts:

    export type SubscriberAndCallbacksFor<
  M extends MethodsOrOptions,
  Q extends QueryMethods = any
> = {
  subscribe: Watcher<StateFor<M>>['subscribe'];
  getState: () => { prev: StateFor<M>; current: StateFor<M> };
  actions: CallbacksFor<M>;
  query: QueryCallbacksFor<Q>;
  history: History;
};

    Components (or external listeners) call store.subscribe(callback). When state changes, all subscribers are notified with the state patch.

    Why This Matters for Vector

    Real-time Git updates require pushing changes to the UI without polling. A pub/sub model enables:

    • Webhook handler: When GitHub sends a commit event, the webhook calls actions.addCommit(data).
    • Immer produces a patch.
    • Store notifies all subscribers.
    • Graph renderer receives the patch, updates only the affected nodes.
    • No polling, no full re-renders, ~50ms latency.

    Event Handling & Dispatch

    Craft's Approach

    Craft uses an event context and declarative event handlers. From Events.tsx:

    export const Events = ({ children }: EventsProps) => {
  const store = useContext(EditorContext);
  const handler = useMemo(
    () => store.query.getOptions().handlers(store),
    [store]
  );
  return (
    <EventHandlerContext.Provider value={handler}>
      <RenderEditorIndicator />
      {children}
    </EventHandlerContext.Provider>
  );
};

    This allows:

    • Custom event handlers: Different event strategies (mouse, touch, drag) can be plugged in.
    • Memoization: Handler is recreated only when store changes, avoiding unnecessary re-renders.
    • Indication system: Real-time visual feedback (drag shadows, resize handles) without mutating the DOM directly.

    Why This Matters for Vector

    Git graph interaction (zoom, pan, search, filter) requires fast event handling. Craft's pattern enables:

    • Pluggable event handlers: Mobile (touch) vs desktop (mouse) can have different drag/zoom logic.
    • Cached handlers: No event listener churn.
    • Visual feedback: Hover effects, branch highlighting, commit details tooltips all driven by events.

    Application to Vector Git Integration

    Proposed Pattern for Git Graph State

    // Git graph state (analogous to Craft's EditorState)
interface GitGraphState {
  commits: Record<CommitSha, Commit>; // All commits in the graph
  branches: Record<BranchName, Branch>; // Branch pointers
  deployments: Deployment[]; // Staging, prod markers
  repos: Record<RepoName, RepoStatus>; // Multi-repo info
  filters: {
    author: Set<string>;
    branch: Set<string>;
    searchText: string;
  };
  ui: {
    selected: CommitSha | null;
    hovered: CommitSha | null;
    zoom: number;
    pan: { x: number; y: number };
  };
}

// Actions (mutations)
const GitGraphActions = (state: GitGraphState, query: GitGraphQueries) => ({
  addCommit: (commit: Commit) => {
    state.commits[commit.sha] = commit;
    // Update branch pointer
    if (state.branches[commit.branch]) {
      state.branches[commit.branch].head = commit.sha;
    }
  },
  deployToStaging: (commitSha: CommitSha) => {
    state.deployments.push({ commitSha, env: 'staging', timestamp: now() });
  },
  filterByAuthor: (author: string) => {
    state.filters.author.add(author);
  },
  setZoom: (z: number) => {
    state.ui.zoom = z;
  },
});

// Queries (read-only)
const GitGraphQueries = {
  getVisibleCommits: () => 
    Object.values(state.commits).filter(c => 
      matches(c, state.filters)
    ),
  getBranchHead: (branch: string) => 
    state.branches[branch]?.head,
  getDeploymentStatus: (repo: string) => 
    state.deployments
      .filter(d => d.repo === repo)
      .sort((a, b) => b.timestamp - a.timestamp)[0],
};

    Webhook Integration

    // GitHub webhook listener (Node.js)
app.post('/webhook/github', (req, res) => {
  const event = req.body;
  if (event.action === 'opened' && event.pull_request) {
    store.actions.addCommit({
      sha: event.pull_request.head.sha,
      branch: event.pull_request.head.ref,
      author: event.pull_request.user.login,
      message: event.pull_request.title,
      timestamp: new Date(),
    });
    // Immer produces patch, subscribers notified ~5ms
  }
});

    React Component (Graph Renderer)

    // Graph component subscribed to state changes
const GitGraph = () => {
  const [state, setState] = useState(() => store.getState().current);
  
  useEffect(() => {
    const unsubscribe = store.subscribe((patch) => {
      // Only re-render if commits, deployments, or filters changed
      if (patch.some(p => 
        p.path[0] === 'commits' || 
        p.path[0] === 'deployments' ||
        p.path[0] === 'filters'
      )) {
        setState(store.getState().current);
      }
    });
    return unsubscribe;
  }, []);
  
  const visibleCommits = store.query.getVisibleCommits();
  
  return (
    <svg>
      {visibleCommits.map(commit => (
        <CommitNode key={commit.sha} commit={commit} />
      ))}
    </svg>
  );
};
